retrieved.text: Have you ever come across the phrase ‘prüfungsaktiv’ or ‘number of exams taken’ and wondered what it means? In a nutshell: Several years ago, a new university financing scheme was introduced in Austria. The Republic of Austria provides funding to the public universities and, thus, also funds a large part of your studies. The allocation of financial resources to universities in Austria is based on the number of students being 'prüfungsaktiv' i.e. students taking a certain number of exams at the relevant university. Bachelor’s, master’s and diploma programmes in which a student completes at least 16 ECTS credits or at least 8 SWS within the academic year are considered 'prüfungsaktiv'. In the academic year of 2020/2021, 53,000 students in total took a certain number of exams at the University of Vienna. The target to be achieved is set every three years between the ministry and the university. Every ECTS credit counts: for you personally and for the University of Vienna Taking exams and writing papers is part of studying. They contribute to your personal success and allow you to progress in your studies. If you take a certain number of exams in your degree programme(s), you also contribute to more funding being allocated to the University of Vienna for innovative research and high-quality teaching. Students who obtain at least 16 ECTS credits in their degree programme per academic year have taken a certain number of exams and are thus considered 'prüfungaktiv’. This is relevant in bachelor’s, diploma and master's programmes. It does not apply to doctoral programmes. Only ECTS credits of courses passed are considered in the calculation. All degree programmes in which students take a certain number of exams are taken into account. - Do all earned ECTS credits count towards examination activity? ECTS credits from recognitions are not taken into account for the calculation. For cooperative degree programmes, only the share of ECTS credits that counts towards examination activity at the University of Vienna is considered. - Why is the number of students who are ‘prüfungsaktiv’ (taking a certain number of exams) important? The more students are ‘prüfungsaktiv’ in all their degree programmes, the more financial resources the University can use for research and teaching. In addition, evidence of having taking a certain number of exams can be a prerequisite for receiving financial aids, such as Austrian family allowance. - What is the difference between the minimum number of credits and the number of students taking a certain number of exams? retrieved.title: Non-degree programme to make up for qualitative admission requirements
heading_path: Consequences of a non-degree programme
retrieved.text:
- Examination attempts in the non-degree programme also count for the degree programme. If all examination attempts are graded negatively in the non-degree programme, you are permanently excluded from the degree programme and cannot be admitted to this programme at the University of Vienna.
- You have to pay the [tuition fee as well as the Students' Union fee](/en/tuition-fee/), irrespective of your citizenship.
- You are not admitted to a specific degree programme, but to attending individual courses ("Besuch einzelner Lehrveranstaltungen").
- The admission to a non-degree programme is possibile until the end of the admission period. However, the registration deadlines for courses end before the beginning of the semester in some cases. Therefore, it is possible that you miss these deadlines.
- Students admitted to a degree programme are favoured in the allocation of course places. If you have any questions regarding the registration for courses and examinations, please contact the [StudiesServiceCenter or StudiesServiceUnit](https://www.univie.ac.at/en/about-us/governance-structure/organisation-of-studies-courses/) of the degree programme in which the courses are offered.
- You are only allowed to take individual examinations/courses and must comply with the curriuclum's registration requirements. Furthermore, you are not allowed to take any public defenses. Graduating is not possible.
- There might be restrictions/limitations when it comes to health insurance, residence permit and financial assistance. Please make an inquiry at the responsible authority about the consequences of your non-degree programme up front.

retrieved.title: What is Artificial Intelligence?
heading_path: Risks associated with the use of AI
What implications does this have for the use of AI in my Studies?
retrieved.text:
- It is important to note that generarive tools such as ChatGPT are not intended for use as research tools and should not be relied upon as reliable sources of knowledge.
- They cannot substitute specialist literature and may also provide incorrect and inaccurate information.
- They may continue, rephrase or summarise texts, but there is no guarantee that they are factually correct.
- Risk of plagiarism: Due to the way AI tools generate text, existing content may be reproduced without alteration or only slightly modified without citing sources.

It is imperative that all statements made with the assistance of AI are meticulously reviewed and verified using reliable sources. Students must assume full responsibility for the academic integrity of their work, including its content, arguments and results.

Procedure in case of student accidents
retrieved.text: What should I do if a fellow student or I have an accident while studying? Accidents have to be reported. Please note that accident reports concerning students in connection with their studies must be submitted electronically. We ask you to please NO longer use any previous PDF forms or copy templates that you may have saved. The most recent version of the form “Accident report for persons in educational institutions” can be used by students who have had an accident at the university or on the way to or from the university. It is completed via the AUVA website ([auva.at/unfallmeldung](http://auva.at/unfallmeldung)). 1. Students fill out the form and select the option “Forward at the educational institution”. 2. Students enter the name and email address of the director of studies. If the accident occurred in a service facility, e.g. in the university library, USI, etc., then the email goes to the DLE management there. In cases of doubt, the report can also be sent to the management of the DLE Study Services and Teaching ([slw.leitung@univie.ac.at](mailto:slw.leitung@univie.ac.at)). 3. The head of the department or the head of the division will be notified and will then forward this form electronically to the AUVA. 4. If you have any questions about the accident report, the AUVA service team will be happy to help at any time. Contact the AUVA at: E-Mail: [wla@auva.at](mailto:wla@auva.at), Tel.: +43 5 9393

retrieved.title: Studying in the University Preparation Programme of the Vienna Universities (VWU)
heading_path: Important note for students in the VWU
retrieved.text:
Once you have been admitted to the University Preparation Programme of the Vienna Universities as a non-degree student, only the supplementary examination in German (Ergänzungsprüfung Deutsch, EPD)  is accepted as a proof of German language proficiency! It is not possible to leave the preparation programme and take a German language exam elsewhere. Disregard may lead to severe consequences such as the loss of your residence permit.

Persons who have quit the VWU and have passed a different German language exam in the meanwhile can still take the supplementary examination in German (EPD).

[Detailed information on the regulation](/en/admission/german-language-proficiency/detailed-information/)

While you are admitted to the non-degree programme "attending the VWU" ("Besuch des Vorstudienlehrgangs"), you are only allowed to attend courses in the VWU and complete the prescribed supplementary examinations there. Taking exams from the degree programme is not allowed.

heading_path: Prüfungsaktiv – examination activity – what does this mean?
reranked.text: Have you ever come across the phrase ‘prüfungsaktiv’ or ‘number of exams taken’ and wondered what it means? In a nutshell: Several years ago, a new university financing scheme was introduced in Austria. The Republic of Austria provides funding to the public universities and, thus, also funds a large part of your studies. The allocation of financial resources to universities in Austria is based on the number of students being 'prüfungsaktiv' i.e. students taking a certain number of exams at the relevant university. Bachelor’s, master’s and diploma programmes in which a student completes at least 16 ECTS credits or at least 8 SWS within the academic year are considered 'prüfungsaktiv'. In the academic year of 2020/2021, 53,000 students in total took a certain number of exams at the University of Vienna. The target to be achieved is set every three years between the ministry and the university. Every ECTS credit counts: for you personally and for the University of Vienna Taking exams and writing papers is part of studying. They contribute to your personal success and allow you to progress in your studies. reranked.title: Non-degree programme to make up for qualitative admission requirements
heading_path: Consequences of a non-degree programme
reranked.text:
- Examination attempts in the non-degree programme also count for the degree programme. If all examination attempts are graded negatively in the non-degree programme, you are permanently excluded from the degree programme and cannot be admitted to this programme at the University of Vienna.
- You have to pay the [tuition fee as well as the Students' Union fee](/en/tuition-fee/), irrespective of your citizenship.
- You are not admitted to a specific degree programme, but to attending individual courses ("Besuch einzelner Lehrveranstaltungen").
- The admission to a non-degree programme is possibile until the end of the admission period. However, the registration deadlines for courses end before the beginning of the semester in some cases. Therefore, it is possible that you miss these deadlines.
- Students admitted to a degree programme are favoured in the allocation of course places. If you have any questions regarding the registration for courses and examinations, please contact the [StudiesServiceCenter or StudiesServiceUnit](https://www.univie.ac.at/en/about-us/governance-structure/organisation-of-studies-courses/) of the degree programme in which the courses are offered.
- You are only allowed to take individual examinations/courses and must comply with the curriuclum's registration requirements. Furthermore, you are not allowed to take any public defenses. Graduating is not possible.
- There might be restrictions/limitations when it comes to health insurance, residence permit and financial assistance. Please make an inquiry at the responsible authority about the consequences of your non-degree programme up front.

reranked.title: What is Artificial Intelligence?
heading_path: Risks associated with the use of AI
What implications does this have for the use of AI in my Studies?
reranked.text:
- It is important to note that generarive tools such as ChatGPT are not intended for use as research tools and should not be relied upon as reliable sources of knowledge.
- They cannot substitute specialist literature and may also provide incorrect and inaccurate information.
- They may continue, rephrase or summarise texts, but there is no guarantee that they are factually correct.
- Risk of plagiarism: Due to the way AI tools generate text, existing content may be reproduced without alteration or only slightly modified without citing sources.

It is imperative that all statements made with the assistance of AI are meticulously reviewed and verified using reliable sources. Students must assume full responsibility for the academic integrity of their work, including its content, arguments and results.

For courses with continuous assessment (e.g. seminars, exercises) the following status values may appear: - Prerequisite currently not met (Voraussetzung noch nicht erfüllt): You have started but not completed your registration or you do not meet the course prerequisites yet. In case you do not want to attend, you have to deregister in due time. - On waiting list (Auf Warteliste): You have not been assigned a place on the course. reranked.title: Studying safely
heading_path: Studying safely on-site.
Procedure in case of student accidents
reranked.text: What should I do if a fellow student or I have an accident while studying? Accidents have to be reported. Please note that accident reports concerning students in connection with their studies must be submitted electronically. We ask you to please NO longer use any previous PDF forms or copy templates that you may have saved. The most recent version of the form “Accident report for persons in educational institutions” can be used by students who have had an accident at the university or on the way to or from the university. It is completed via the AUVA website ([auva.at/unfallmeldung](http://auva.at/unfallmeldung)). 1. Students fill out the form and select the option “Forward at the educational institution”. 2. Students enter the name and email address of the director of studies. If the accident occurred in a service facility, e.g. in the university library, USI, etc., then the email goes to the DLE management there. In cases of doubt, the report can also be sent to the management of the DLE Study Services and Teaching ([slw.leitung@univie.ac.at](mailto:slw.leitung@univie.ac.at)). 3. The head of the department or the head of the division will be notified and will then forward this form electronically to the AUVA. 4. If you have any questions about the accident report, the AUVA service team will be happy to help at any time. Contact the AUVA at: E-Mail: [wla@auva.at](mailto:wla@auva.at), Tel.: +43 5 9393

reranked.title: Studying in the University Preparation Programme of the Vienna Universities (VWU)
heading_path: Important note for students in the VWU
reranked.text:
Once you have been admitted to the University Preparation Programme of the Vienna Universities as a non-degree student, only the supplementary examination in German (Ergänzungsprüfung Deutsch, EPD)  is accepted as a proof of German language proficiency! It is not possible to leave the preparation programme and take a German language exam elsewhere. Disregard may lead to severe consequences such as the loss of your residence permit.

Persons who have quit the VWU and have passed a different German language exam in the meanwhile can still take the supplementary examination in German (EPD).

[Detailed information on the regulation](/en/admission/german-language-proficiency/detailed-information/)

While you are admitted to the non-degree programme "attending the VWU" ("Besuch des Vorstudienlehrgangs"), you are only allowed to attend courses in the VWU and complete the prescribed supplementary examinations there. Taking exams from the degree programme is not allowed.

selected.title: FAQs about being 'prüfungsaktiv' (examination activity)
heading_path: Prüfungsaktiv – examination activity – what does this mean?
selected.text: Have you ever come across the phrase ‘prüfungsaktiv’ or ‘number of exams taken’ and wondered what it means? In a nutshell: Several years ago, a new university financing scheme was introduced in Austria. The Republic of Austria provides funding to the public universities and, thus, also funds a large part of your studies. The allocation of financial resources to universities in Austria is based on the number of students being 'prüfungsaktiv' i.e. students taking a certain number of exams at the relevant university. Bachelor’s, master’s and diploma programmes in which a student completes at least 16 ECTS credits or at least 8 SWS within the academic year are considered 'prüfungsaktiv'. In the academic year of 2020/2021, 53,000 students in total took a certain number of exams at the University of Vienna. The target to be achieved is set every three years between the ministry and the university. Every ECTS credit counts: for you personally and for the University of Vienna Taking exams and writing papers is part of studying. They contribute to your personal success and allow you to progress in your studies. If you take a certain number of exams in your degree programme(s), you also contribute to more funding being allocated to the University of Vienna for innovative research and high-quality teaching. Therefore, the University of Vienna strives to ensure that you pass many exams. Your directors of

selected.text: exams. # FAQs about being 'prüfungsaktiv' (examination activity) / - When are students ‘prüfungsaktiv’? Students who obtain at least 16 ECTS credits in their degree programme per academic year have taken a certain number of exams and are thus considered 'prüfungaktiv’. This is relevant in bachelor’s, diploma and master's programmes. It does not apply to doctoral programmes. Only ECTS credits of courses passed are considered in the calculation. All degree programmes in which students take a certain number of exams are taken into account. - Do all earned ECTS credits count towards examination activity? ECTS credits from recognitions are not taken into account for the calculation. For cooperative degree programmes, only the share of ECTS credits that counts towards examination activity at the University of Vienna is considered. - Why is the number of students who are ‘prüfungsaktiv’ (taking a certain number of exams) important? The more students are ‘prüfungsaktiv’ in all their degree programmes, the more financial resources the University can use for research and teaching. In addition, evidence of having taking a certain number of exams can be a prerequisite for receiving financial aids, such as Austrian family allowance. - What is the difference between the minimum number of credits and the number of students taking a certain number of exams? Have you ever come across the phrase ‘prüfungsaktiv’ or ‘number of exams taken’ and wondered what it means? In a nutshell: Several years ago, a new university financing scheme was introduced in Austria. The Republic of Austria provides funding to the public universities and, thus, also funds a large part of your studies. The allocation of financial resources to universities in Austria is based on the number of students being 'prüfungsaktiv' i.e. students taking a certain number of exams at the relevant university. Bachelor’s, master’s and diploma programmes in which a student completes at least 16 ECTS credits or at least 8 SWS within the academic year are considered 'prüfungsaktiv'. In the academic year of 2020/2021, 53,000 students in total took a certain number of exams at the University of Vienna. The target to be achieved is set every three years between the ministry and the university. Every ECTS credit counts: for you personally and for the University of Vienna Taking exams and writing papers is part of studying. They contribute to your personal success and allow you to progress in your studies. If you take a certain number of exams in your degree programme(s), you also contribute to more funding being allocated to the University of Vienna for innovative research and high-quality teaching.
